author: @bxxj
text: It is also important to note  that rivets are "immune " to vibrational loosening making them ideal for farm equipment and aircraft structural components and attaching skins to the support structure. We see "rivits" every time we use a pair of lineman pliers or surgical tools like hemostats and sisors. The Hinge joint is made by peaning over the Hinge pin just like a rivit.

author: @anneinfurna8528
text:
I have used and have made rivits a lot. One easy way to get brass dome headed rivits of virtually any length and diameter is to search out Brass lineoleum nails. Sometimes called 'Escutcheon Pins', Just nip off the tips and wa la -- a Brass rivit. an easy way to make a dome faced punch is to hold a steel domed rivit in a vise on your Drill press, and come down directly on it with a piece of steel rod that is heated up
to yellow hot on the tip chucked in the drill chuck -- come down on the cool rivit head and press hard -- It will take on the conical shape that way. You can Dress grind it to shape after that and heat it up and temper it, or harden it from there.  Rivits are Fun to do and Ez!

author: @veeanon7231
text: I've milled screws and bolts to size before

author: @veeanon7231
text: Great stuff rob just dont use sub standard rivets to build "unsinkable" ships 😉

author: @jpsimon206
text: I thought you might find it interesting, there are copper rivets. I don't think they're pure copper, but they're specifically designed for water jackets in nuclear steam engines. It is preferred because of its softness. When copper work hardens and you then heat it, it returns to soft much more consistently than other materials. The idea is that heat cycling will eventually destroy any fastener but the copper is better at being hardened and softened repeatedly

author: @jpsimon206
text: On old bench vises there is very often a pair of holes close to the anvil. I have always wondered what these are for. I've seen them both on top as well as on the side. I have heard two people definitively state that they are for valves. This does not make sense to me, you can hardly lap a valve by hand. Watching this video makes me wonder if they were not more likely designed for rivets. Say two different basic depths. Any thoughts?

author: @jpsimon206
text: While it is not necessarily applicable in rivets the size you were demonstrating, I think it's an important detail to let the rivet cool before using it to join sheet metal. Since the metal expands when hot, it very likely will not fit in the hole anymore until it cools. More relevant is that if you use hot rivets then as they cool and contract, there might not be enough length to hold the two sheets together. Small detail, but gets a lot more relevant when you're using say half inch rivets

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Hum… If it was aluminium riveting brass or mild steel it would be OK but the other way around? I think that the aluminium sheet would corrode around the hole, due to the aluminium being more (a lot more) anodic than the brass.

author: @dav1dbone
text: There's a lot of metal fixing and fixtures largely unknown to most folks, for example, rivnuts -  like fixing threads to pipes - (bike bottle cage), staking - a compression fit, and PEM fasteners which come in a huge array of options. All super handy for the home fabricator/inventor;-)

author: @arb261268
text:
The art of riveting is not dead, all blacksmiths (of which there is a bit of resurgence) use riveting a lot when fixing pieces together not just 2 flat bits of metal, you could rivet 4 x 1/4” square bar together if you need. Knife makers will usually use copper rivets through the handle material and tang or rivet the tang itself if the handle is a through tang to get a very secure connection.
As a side note you would find making rivets a lot easier using a ball peen hammer rather than a flat faced claw with sharp edges.
